title: vimeo_1187879804
displayTitle: 2026-05-14 - Primary Production Committee - Fisheries Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 14 May 2026 | Primary Production Committee Fisheries Amendment Bill NZ Rock lobster Industry Council Mark Edwards, Chief Executive Officer Te Runanga o Ngai Tahu and Ngai Tahu Seafood Tanya Stevens, Senior Policy Advisor Sealord Group Limited Doug Paulin, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Fisheries Amendment Bill. NZ Rock lobster Industry Council. Mark Edwards, Chief Executive Officer

chunks.text: And leave some time for questions. I'm sure the committee will have plenty of questions, so fire away. Good morning, and thanks very much for the opportunity to present. My intent is to talk about sections of the bill that are of key interest to New Zealand's rock lobster sector in less than 10 minutes and leave the remaining time for your questions.

Our submission provides more detail on the bill proposals, what we think they'll achieve, and addresses concerns or issues that have been raised, sets out improvements and amendments we suggest are needed. Overall, some components of the bill make really important enhancements to the Fisheries Act by improving the tools available to manage fisheries, making better use of information, recognizing the industry's role in management, and enabling more targeted and cost-effective approach.

The proposals achieve these outcomes while maintaining sustainability standards, providing for consultation with all sectors, and without undermining the QMS. The additional mechanisms we think enable a more responsive and timely management and increase the number of stocks that can be reviewed annually. Because the provisions also enable careful planning around future decisions, they support investment in research and monitoring because of the clear links between science and evidence and management decisions.

Those improvements increase the certainty around how stocks are managed. There's been considerable comment on the bill, obviously, with views expressed about what stakeholders want from management. We believe that examination of the proposal shows that they will deliver on many of these issues. For example, they maintain and build on the current protections and safeguards in the Act to ensure sustainability of harvest and address adverse effects.

In fact, we think they're likely to improve overall environmental sustainability performance through increasing the capacity of the system to make timely changes to sustainability settings and enabling a more targeted approach to addressing those issues. Now I'll talk briefly about the proposals in the bill that are most important, we think, to improve the management of rock lobster fisheries for all sectors. First up for us is management procedures.

They're a key tool in modern fisheries management, clearly regarded as international best practice. They recognize and provide for the use of-- or recognizing MPs would encourage their use and substantially reduce the time and effort needed to put in place responsive management changes, either decreases where they're necessary or increases. For rock lobster stocks, what that means is that we could review the TAC for most or all rock lobster stocks on an annual basis instead of the current circumstances where normally only two

stocks get reviewed each year. MPs put in place arrangements for the period following a very comprehensive evaluation against targets and limits and stock characteristics supported by full consultation. They support maintaining stocks at management targets, and they outline the information that will be used to inform decisions.

They're not a set and forget arrangement. The breakout rules mean they can be put in place, and those rules specify upfront how they and when they should be revoked if the procedure is not achieving the objectives. They have been used since the mid-1990s for rock lobster fisheries and have, on the record, you can see they've supported considerable rebuild of stocks, 400% increase in abundance overall since that time, and maintenance of stocks generally at management targets. Notwithstanding, there are management issues

that we need to address. The second area we want to emphasize the importance of is recognizing the non-regulatory measures in TAC decisions. Enabling recognition of these measures is an important step forward to support more responsive management and tools that can benefit fisheries for all sectors, such as shelving, spatial management, and area-based catch limits. The rock lobster industry has quite a long record of implementing these sorts of measures.

Currently, there is legal doubt about whether the decision-maker can consider or take into account these measures, leading to uncertainty and an inconsistent approach. In recent years for rock lobster, it's clear that we need to manage at a scale less than the QMA to usefully recognize the performance of parts of a stock within a QMA.

Non-regulatory measures are likely to play an increasingly important role, and they do things that the ministry does not have the tools or the flexibility to do that industry can do on a relatively short-term basis. There are, however, set out in our submission a number of issues we have with the current drafting, and we put forward some suggestions for your consideration. The third area is TAC setting under high, medium, low information provisions.

We think that categorization better recognizes the different levels of information available on stocks and will enable more regular revision of TACs to address sustainability risks and provide for use. This is particularly an issue for medium and low information stocks, where the effect of the current provisions is that it's very difficult to review those stocks, and the outcome is they remain unchanged for long periods, even decades.

Clearly, that's not good fisheries management, and this is one mechanism that will kind of unlock that a bit. Clarifying the role of the TAC, fourth area we think is useful. We do support the idea that the TAC is primarily directed at managing stock abundance at a quite a management area level rather than addressing sub-QMA management issues or adverse effects of fishing.

TAC is not very effective or appropriate to address those sorts of issues. The way that it's set up in the bill, the decisions need to take into account the effect on other stocks, the interaction between stocks, and the environmental conditions affecting the stock. We think they are the right considerations to influence the setting of a TAC along with the underpinning science.For other issues within the Q&A, other more direct mechanisms will be more effective, targeted, and more transparent, impose lower costs overall.

Another area we support is the considerations for TAC decisions for Section 13 stocks. The bill sets out this new comprehensive list of standard factors that must be had regard to for Section 13 TAC decisions. We think these provide useful consolidated guidance for decision-makers, but we suggest that biological characteristics of the stock should be added to the list of standard factors, and that we should retain the take into account consideration for fisheries plans that's lost through the amendment of 112A.

We also support the multi-year TAC decisions because the provisions provide for establishing arrangements that create certainty for stakeholders. They set in place like an MP at sort of a low level MP, set in place a regime that can manage a stock for successive years after carefully considering its circumstances.

It allows other stocks to be reviewed and increases the capacity of a system. It allows for adjustments where there is insufficient information to support a permanent change. Carry forward. The bill provides for an application for additional ACE carry forward in exception circumstances.

Certainly, the rock lobster industry has experienced situation where ACE can't be taken for an economic return in situations that are really unrelated to stock sustainability. This can cause enormous economic stress and costs on fishing businesses and families. A clear example is the closure of the Chinese market in 2020 as a result of COVID. But other ones include biotoxin outbreaks to address food safety risks, which have no impact on the lobsters, but they just prevent the ACE being taken. And they're increasingly common

in spatial extent. We don't believe there are issues with the ACE carry forward. It doesn't result in more fish being caught. It simply allows some of the ACE from year one to be taken in year two. We do think the criteria in the Act need to be expanded, because they don't take into account some relevant considerations. For example, they wouldn't deal with the current fuel crisis. If diesel price extended to an extent where you had to tie up boats, the current criteria in the Act would not allow for carry forward in those circumstances.

And some other human-induced events. Our submission sets out more detail on that bill. So I'm going to stop there. There are, of course, other provisions we're interested in, but I wanted to leave lots of time for questions. Thank you very much. Rachel. Thank you, Mark. My question is around that ACE carry forward, and you've mentioned an example. I guess there's two examples that I think are quite relevant if you could talk the committee through.

One is around what happened with COVID, but also Cyclone Gabrielle. And I guess the differences in those two scenarios and why ACE carry forward was useful in one but not the other. So if you could talk us through that, it'd be really helpful. Okay. Well, we'll start with Gabrielle. I remember when I started to get the phone calls and the emails, I think 28, 29 January, 2020, it- COVID.

For COVID. So very... What did I say? Gabrielle. Gabrielle. Sorry. COVID. I was thinking that doesn't sound like Gabrielle. Yeah. That's right. Sorry. So it became very clear quickly that Chinese market was gonna close, and it did. So that's at a fairly important point of the fishing year for rock lobster because Chinese New Year normally occurs end of January, beginning of February.

So there was a lot of uncaught ACE. Now, in this case, the government reacted fairly quickly and using the existing carry forward provisions, they took rock lobster off the fifth schedule and allowed carry forward to apply. Did it in the nick of time. In fact, the decision was only communicated, I think, well into March.

What percentage was it, just quickly? Sorry? What percentage was allowed to carry? 10. The current provisions allow up to 10%. So the lesser of under catch or 10%, whichever is the lesser. So we do think there are circumstances where larger amounts should be considered. We've talked about the economic stress that not being able to take ACE can cause for people who've paid a significant cost for it and, for example, can't get an economic return. I mean, the fuel crisis is one of those things.

If diesel price were to go beyond $4 or $5, clearly a lot of boats would need to tie up. It's got nothing to do with the sustainability of the fishery, but it is a kind of a self-mechanism to allow for... It doesn't involve the government and compensating or assistance. It just allows those catching rights from year one to be carried forward into year two.

And Gabrielle, can you just quickly talk about why you didn't do it in Gabrielle? So Gabrielle's, we think, quite a different situation and makes clear why it's important that the provisions and decision-making around carry forward clearly consider sustainability. So the way the provisions in the new bill are set up, when an application is received, it must be supported by 75% of QSOs.

The decision-maker has to consider whether there is any sustainability risk. So the issue of carry forward was raised following the 2023 cyclones in Cray three, but here we have a stock that is at a low point. Clearly, there are sustainability issues. We've had three TAC reductions, and industry has shelved twice in the last two years, and currently we've put in place spatial management measures to deal with the lower performance in an area of the stock. So when that discussion came up, there was no support within the industry to use carry forward

provisions in that circumstance because it was quite clear that although the weatherExtreme weather events had affected ability to fish. They had also affected sustainability of the stock. Mm-hmm. That area of the coastline north of Gisborne is very prone to sedimentation and so on, and that sedimentation in the inshore environment affects lobsters to an extent that we don't know long term.

But very clearly there were sustainability risks, so no proposal was even advanced. The discussion with the industry agreed that that was not appropriate. But the bill, as I said, provides a protection in that space, giving the decision-maker discretion if an application should come forward to decline it on the basis there are sustainability issues.

Mm-hmm. Susan. No, my questions have been answered. So it was about the carry forward. Thank you very much. Okay. Steve. Yeah, mine's in a similar space. It's around the environmental considerations. So the catch limit's usually not the most effective or appropriate tool to address adverse effects on fishing or fine-scale management issues.

But you're notwithstanding that catch limits are an effective tool, it's just that it's not the only thing that should be being considered. Yeah, of course. I mean, the fundamental of the QMS is you have a control on total removals from the fishery- Yep... that maintains the stock at its management targets and avoids breaching any sustainability limits. What we're saying is that if you're trying to address, deal with a local issue, for example, an adverse effect caused by a method in a particular location, you can indirectly try and address that method by culling down the whole

TAC, C, and reducing catch overall. But clearly the more effective mechanism is to introduce measures that restrict the use of that method in the area where it's causing the risk or the habitat damage. And what the, I think the provisions in the bill attempt to do is shift the emphasis on setting the TAC to address the overall sustainability output control issue, and direct the decision-makers towards using more targeted and effective measures for, localized abundance or fine-scale management effects.

Very so. Steve. My sup is, I mean, the essence of what that alludes to is that we take a broader ecological view of the whole fishery, which is that, um, there's, as you rightly point out, there's interactions across species and across circumstances in a fishery.

Um, who is best placed to make that evaluation of overall ecological health, and therefore assist in determining management strategies or, uh, T- TAC in the context of a broader ecological view? Well, there's clearly the Act sets up the regulator and the ultimate decision-maker as having the authority in that space to decide on what measures are necessary to r- reach the Act's ecosystem considerations.

Yeah. The Fisheries Act, although 1996, even in retrospect, still stands the test of time as having relatively good set of requirements to address environmental considerations and effect an ecosystem-based approach. The environmental principles have stood the test of time. They're quite well formulated. That doesn't mean that there's not room for improvement or that we don't need more information in some circumstances to better inform, um, the way we address those ecosystem effects.

But considering the interaction between, um, species and the environmental impacts of, um, the stock and the effects of things like climate change, they are all relevant considerations. And you can see for, speaking for rock lobster fisheries, over the last decade and maybe particularly the last five years, we can see a big shift in management and the way that decisions are made to take those things into account. Um, so it- it- it's happening.

Can I just sup off, um, Mr. Chair, can I just sup off your, the answer to the first part of the question, which was around those targeted measures? 'Cause you talked about TAC essentially being a blunt tool. Mm-hmm. What are some... Can you give us some examples, um, of what those targeted measures would be if you're looking at like sub-QMS, for example. So it's a particular area within the tech- within the area that's- Okay... That's, yeah.

Sure. Yeah. Uh, and there are a number of examples. But, uh, so, so if we go back to Cray three- Yeah... you know, which is currently a fishery that we're looking at very closely because it's clearly, it's, it's depleted, and we want to make sure that it rebuilds, uh, and so on. So part of the considerations last year was looking again at the stock assessment, and although it showed there was a, you know, a slight positive trend, it wasn't the sort of rebuild that was wanted. So we looked at a- another reduction.

We considered shelving, but thought that we supported a reduction overall as being necessary. So we put forward the proposal for a 30% reduction. But the problem is, is that the, the, the biological performance of region one and region two, the north and the south of Cray three, they're quite different. They've got different stock characteristics.

That's not something that government has the tools to deal with very easily. So in discussion with the scientists, what the industry formulated was an ace spreading arrangement whereby we agreed to essentially shift effort within the QMA to reduce pressure on the, you know, the least performing part of the stock. The minister in this case accepted that as a, a proposal and was part of his decision on Cray three, along with the TAC reduction. In Cray one, where we've got issues on the east coast of Northland, Cray one, um, suggested a closure

of, um, the area on the east coast of Northland. That's now been, um, overtaken by the government's decision to stop commercial and recreational fishing in the Hauraki Gulf, where again, it was recognized there are abundance issues. Cray two put forward an area to severely restrain commercial catch, essentially have a sub-area catch limit in the Hauraki Gulf.

So within those Cray areas, you basically said, "Here's the piece within Hauraki Gulf with depletion, so we're going to target that area, not the overall-" Correct. Yeah. Um, one final question from Susan. I, I just quickly wanted to talk about the time limit for the judicial review. You mentioned that you weren't actually that excited about that, that it was lumping everything in together. So, um, I just, just wondering if you could talk about your- General concerns about the time limit and that it might lead to court action

That's right. We understand, I guess, the sentiment behind it, but judicial review is an important part of the process in New Zealand, and we think that such a short time limit would potentially incentivize people to lodge legal action, where if they had more time to consider it, it wouldn't be necessary.

Right. There is also issues that for all parties, that if you want to look carefully at a decision within that sort of timeframe, you wouldn't even be able to get all of the information behind what supported a decision and properly inform your view. So in our view, trying to have some sort of time limit is appropriate.

We suggest, as you can see in our submission, a longer one, but that time limit is, we don't believe it's appropriate. Thank you, gentlemen. Thank you. Time flies when you're having fun. And I really appreciate your thoughts and submission. Yeah. I think it's- Yeah. Thanks... really, really useful for the committee. Thank you for your time.

Yeah. Thanks for the opportunity. I will say that we did make a number of recommendations for amendments and adjustments, but there just hasn't been time today, but I'm sure the committee may have time or- Yes. Yeah. We're aware of it... the officials. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Right. What we'll do, folks, is have Ngāi Tahu next, but what I would like to do is seek leave from the committee to change the agenda because we've got Doug Paulin, CEO of Sealord.

It's been a bit of a muck up. He's keen to speak to the committee, even if it's for 10 to 15 minutes, so if we could have him- Yeah... afterwards. Is that okay? Fine. Yeah. Thank you. Right. If we could bring- The guys are online. Are they? Excellent. We can bring them in. I'm going to have to leave in 20 minutes. Can the clerks please give us a copy of Sealord's submission?
Yes. Quite quickly. Thank you. Yeah.

How many individuals have it? Good morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. If you could just introduce yourselves and then we'll leave it with you. You have 20 minutes. Be really good if you could leave some time for questions.

You can hear us, can you? Yes, we can. Kia ora koutou. All right. It's in your hands. Kia ora, and thank you. Just wanted to acknowledge you, Miles, as the chair, and also thank you to the other panel members. Appreciate you giving us the time to talk to the submission.

Just to briefly introduce ourselves. My name is Aaron Endacott. I'm here representing Ngāi Tahu Seafood. My colleague beside me is Tania Stevens. She's here representing Tirunaga o Ngāi Tahu. And we also have online Rodney Tribe, who I'm hoping is also able to join as a panelist, and he's here to assist us to answer any questions the panel may have. Rodney's been with Ngāi Tahu Seafood for over 30 years, so has extensive experience within the industry.

Yeah, I see he's just jumped on. Thank you for that. We would also like to acknowledge those who've gone before us, those who stand with us, those who helped through our settlement process and our children after us. This is a joint submission by Tirunaga o Ngāi Tahu and Ngāi Tahu Seafood.

Tirunaga o Ngāi Tahu is made up of 18 Papatipu Runanga, and Ngāi Tahu Seafood is the commercial operation which has been set up to manage the fisheries assets that came through settlement. The economic return that we generate goes back to the iwi to fulfill a number of aspirations the iwi have, including whānau wellbeing, education, health, et cetera.

Ngāi Tahu generally supports the changes in this bill, as you will see in our submission. And our approach to the submission today is to talk through some of the items and thinking that underpins our submission, and then give a bit of a summary, and then allow some time at the end for questions that the panel may have.

And I'll hand over to Tania to talk through the Tirunaga o Ngāi Tahu portions. Well, kia ora koutou. Thanks, Aaron. So I've listened to some of your previous submissions that you've received, and I'm really conscious that the committee is receiving and hearing a range of strong views about both the bill and commercial fisheries more generally.

So what we want to do is take the opportunity to really anchor the, no pun intended, the matters raised in our submission as foundational to your consideration of both the submissions and the bill. While you may receive fewer submissions that touch on these points, the matters raised by Ngāi Tahu, Te Ohu Kaimoana and others engage really fundamental rights and obligations that shouldn't be wasted on that basis. So Ngāi Tahu are seafaring people, and we have a long tradition of exercising kaitiakitanga alongside mahi kai, so working the kai.

And this is as true now as it was 800 years ago or so. And that relationship is seamless for us. So as noted in our submission, fishing is a right for Māori, and that was acknowledged in Article 2 of the Treaty of Waitangi. So with the introduction of the Quota Management System, the QMS, Māori saw those rights being publicly commercialized and sold. But with the Crown, and following extensive negotiation, we landed in a place where we could accept the QMSAnd it cannot be overstated how important the Fisheries Act is to upholding the fisheries settlement.

And therefore, how important it is that the act can continue to adapt and move forward. And these considerations underline our reasons for supporting the bill. And Aaron will build on this further. Kia ora, Tania. As Tania mentioned, our submission is underpinned by our Article 2 rights, which were confirmed in settlement. Also our obligation as kaitiaki.

But we are also privileged to be operational within the seafood industry and the commercial industry. So we also have experience within that space which informs some of our pieces of our submission. Operations wise, we are operational within rock lobster, pāua, and a lot of wet fish species, either directly or through arrangements with commercial partners in the industry.

Our operations are across mainly Te Waipounamu, but we do have some footprint in other areas. Our workforce is around about 60 staff. That does fluctuate seasonally depending on the busy times of the season.

We are privileged through that process to be able to sit on a lot of advocation groups and industry management bodies where we can push our views forward, and we can ensure that our values are pushed into those forums. We actively take advice from fishermen and listen to the fishing sector and bring those views forward into those forums also.

So it gives us an ability to understand how important the industry-led proactive measures that are put in place have been beneficial to fisheries, which is why we're supportive of the non-regulatory measures having increased weight when decisions are made about the future.

Our experience in the fishing industry, although we note there are isolated instances of poor performance, generally the vast majority of the engagements we have are always advocating for sustainable outcomes and for a sustainable fishery that is here for the future generations, and that really is encouraging to us as active members within this space.

Ngāi Tahu and Ngāi Tahu Seafood, as Tania mentioned, we really believe in the integrity of the settlement process. We believe in the integrity of the quota management system. And we are supportive of the bill's intentions to improve efficiency, to improve some flexibility in decision making.

And we believe it will enhance the ability to have sustainable fisheries going into the future. In summary, our submission is generally in support of the bill with some caveats in there which you'll be able to read and ask questions on. We would also like to highlight that any changes to the bill and the ability to administrate those changes needs to be appropriately resourced by MPI.

Our experience with some fisheries is not necessarily a process one, but could be a resource one, where the process was late to be implemented due to resourcing. And that has impacted a fishery, which is also why we advocate that the industry or proactive measures from industry are just as important to managing fisheries and why we support that extra weight.

And we'll leave it there and just would like to take some time to allow the panel to ask us some questions on our submission. Thank you very much. We've got a question from Steve Abel. Kia ora koutou. Recognizing your customary rights to the Moana and Article 2 rights, acknowledge that absolutely.

How do you manage the tensions between what we have observed in terms of certain fishery collapses around hoki or orange roughy and appropriate long-term protection of the health of those species, when what is, I think, not unfairly observed as certain commercial fishing practices are having a long-term deleterious effect on those fisheries.

I wonder how Ngāi Tahu deals with the tensions between the kaitiaki responsibilities you outlined and the commercial fishing side of things. Yeah, I can answer that. I think with the orange roughy, that is probably more of a historical pre-QMS issue that was, we believe, helped by the introduction of the QMS system.

But the question around managing tension is a good one because our feet are often held to the fire by our own whānau and our own papatipuWhich is why we continually advocate for sustainable practices. And any time we are aware of any issues that are coming into these fisheries, we are always on those forums advocating our whanau views.

We're not the sole decision-makers in these forums, but we do believe that having our say at those forums is equally as important, and we take those views forward and can feed back that we have submitted those views. But I understand the question, and it is something we regularly deal with in terms of the social license to operate from our own whanau.

I have a bit of a sup on that. So you talked about the non-regulatory measures, and the previous submitter gave us examples of non-regulatory measures in their submission that they feel are working well in the rock lobster industry. Would you be confident that non-regulatory measures would be as successful in other parts of the seafood industry? Because it would seem, you look at some areas in New Zealand, the fisheries are seriously depleted.

Yes, we believe that they would be. We've been involved in many instances of advocating for non-regulatory measures, with some examples being shelving or catch spreading or moving certain catch out of certain areas of a larger fishery. Those measures have been successful and we continually advocate and listen to fishers, some of which are quota owners and are not supportive.

But due to our ability to influence and be operational, we always support and push those measures that we believe are important. We would also like to add that if there is a shelving, that that should potentially limit the ability to roll over that stock, similar to what it would if a TACC reduction was put in place.

But generally speaking, we believe those actions have been beneficial to fisheries in the ones that we're involved in, and think that as a decision-making tool for industry to make proactive decisions prior to having to have MPI involved, by listening to those on the water and those in processing who are closer to the issues, that it does make for an effective tool.

And you may also note from our submission that we've specifically included support for the monitoring provisions and sought to make that stronger. Because monitoring and ensuring implementation of the measure is, we consider to be very important as part of that process. Thanks for that.

We've got a question from Mark Cameron. Good morning, everyone, and kia ora. I'm just interested, the chair mentioned non-regulatory tools in the industry, and you've both advocated for the use of that. There's a natural tension between that and obviously the TAC rules that are implemented by MPI.
How do you gauge when it is necessary to get MPI involved? Are you able to unmute, Rodney?

I'm pretty certain I can. Yeah, you're good. Yep, so very good question. We would actually say that those non-regulatory measures, part of those are for the minister to take account of those. And if I use examples down here in the Deep South, we've had non-regulatory measures in our paua fisheries down here for a number of years. Paua 5A, paua 5D, even paua 5B.

Industry would probably say at some stage we would like those to become regulatory measures. But at the moment, we use them as non-regulatory. So the clash between the two, we look at that and go, industry can actually manage quite well. What we're saying is the minister should take those into account.

Right? So we're not saying use them opposed to, use them in conjunction with. Right. Thank you, Rodney. Got a question from Suz Redmayne. Kia ora. Hi. Just talking about the carryover, which is 7.9 of your submission, and you mentioned at the end that Ngāi Tahu are consulted and that this consultation genuinely informs the decision made. And I just wondered, is that the case now?

And also, I note that sometimes these were... I know it was used during COVID and quick decisions were required, and I just wondered if that might hinder the process whereby quick action is required, or why you want that provision in there. I can start that one. So I think as a general proposition, if carryover is suggested within the Ngāi Tahu takiwā, we would like to be consulted on that.

And then I think certainly as members of the industry, we understand the need to be able to act in a prompt manner. But further to that, maybe Rodney or Aaron, if you wanted to. Yeah, I think in terms of the stocks in our area from a Ngāi Tahu Seafood perspective-Being a quota owner, we would be involved in that decision-making for the majority of the quota, especially in rock lobster and paua in our area. But with other fisheries that we're not so actively involved in, we would like the opportunity to have Te Runanga o Ngāi Tahu consulted, and don't

believe it would cause a timeframe issue, as we are generally very responsive. Okay. Thank you very much. Thank you. There's no further questions, and we've come to time. So, I really appreciate your submission, both the oral and written, and the committee will take these things into consideration. So thank you very much.
Thank you. Thank you. Ka ra.

Right. Moving on. We're going to bring in Sea- Yes... Norwood. Yeah, that's me. Oh, very good. Back. Welcome, and thanks for your patience. We've had a bit of a snafu, as you might be aware. And thanks for being able to fit in anyway. So the floor is yours.

My name's Doug Paulin. I'm the CEO of Sealord Group, and I'm here just to talk briefly about our submission. Mostly, I'd like to leave time for questions. Because the majority of what I say in terms of the things we support in the bill, Mark's actually talked some of that detail when we did the rock lobster submission, notwithstanding that I fish in the deep water, because obviously the Fisheries Act applies across the board.

So very briefly, we believe that managerial and recognizing of procedures that currently industry do which are not recognized within the act are a very positive change. And I'd like to talk about a couple of those in relation to the question around orange roughy and hoki, which was asked briefly of Ngāi Tahu. So when the hoki fishery, when it dropped substantially, prior to that, the science said the hoki fishery was in a great position.

And that is because there is a significant lag, normally two years, between when the science can be analyzed and when it comes through, and then it's acted upon. What we were seeing on the water by our fishers was a reduction in the size of marks and definitely less fish in the water. And so what the industry did was we took the position of dropping it ourselves through shelving initiatives.

We also closed some of the spawning areas for a period of time, and we had rotational closures. Now, all of those things we did as industry were not able to be recognized by the minister, and we had to wait until hoki came on the plenary for the TAC action to be adjusted. And whilst I say that the industries still have some fishers who are not part of the group that agrees those measures, so there were parts of the industry that were still fishing in the spawn boxes and were still catching their full area.

So by having a real recognition of these procedures in a way where the minister can take account of them, we believe it would be a real positive change, as one example. Currently, there are a couple of bycatch fisheries where there are issues. Ling seven is, for example. It won't be looked at by the plenary for a significant period of time.

We're looking at taking some changes as industry to reduce the catches in that fishery. How those are similar, the TAC won't be changed until it's actually part of the plenary. So by having the ability to make changes which are currently not part of how the act operates, we think will be very beneficial for stock management.

In terms of setting TACs across multiple years, we're very positive in regards to that change, not only around certainty, but what it means is that every year you have to look at that stock again. There has to be agreement around what are the things that will require change in terms of that TAC. But what it means is that stocks currently, which might only be looked at once every six or seven years, if you agree, say, a five-year TAC based on what is happening at the time.

You say various things have to be in place, you have to then go and look at it every year. But it doesn't take up the full plenary. You don't have to go to a full submission. There's still consultation, obviously. But it requires the minister to look at it again. Whereas at the moment, it's sort of one change and then not looked at again for potentially five or six years for those medium and low stock.

It's where we don't have a lot of information. So we believe that is an incredibly positive change going forward. Other than that, really, I'm happy to take any questions on the submission or actually anything in the bill, because I believe a lot of those changes impact us all. Thank you very much. We've got a question from Rachel. I've got lots of questions. So I'll start with one, and then I'll see if I can get another one in. But you talked about the information, I guess, in order to make decisions.

And one of the things I'm a bit obsessed about is have we got good enough data and information to be making good decisions? On your submission, item 20, where you talked about pilchards, and there was a year where you, I think it was last year, when you had a massive catch.

Yeah. And I guess, so sure, you had a massive catch, but have you actually got good information on how much stock is still left within the fishery? Because that's, I think, the concern is when you're making decisions to increase catch-Is there good enough information to support that?

Well, thank you. And maybe talk about this particular example a bit more in terms of what other information you've got. Yeah. So I think in terms of the ability to have all the science that you do and say a high information stock, I would say there definitely is not that ability to have that on every stock, just because of the scale and the cost of doing that science. So if you're in an ideal world, every single stock in New Zealand would have the same level of science that Hoki has- Mm... for instance. And that's not going to be possible.

But there are, and this is recognized worldwide, catch per unit effort, and acoustic monitoring of the biomass that's in the water whilst you are catching the fish, is a viable way of recognizing how much fish is there and how much you are catching of that existing biomass and be able to make an in-season adjustment that recognizes for some species, particularly when they are pelagic based and come in and out of the EEZ or some that have massive recruitment events but they happen in singular periods to be able to make those changes

and those tools are available, and are able to be used. Mm-hmm. And that said, that doesn't mean the minister obviously has to make those changes either because at the end of the day you've got to make your application. All factors have to be considered and then the minister would make the decision whether it is or isn't appropriate for a change to be made.

Mm-hmm. So on this, can I just ask this up, on this particular fish stock, have you got information that there's actually an abundance in addition to what you've caught? Yes. So the biomass readings in terms of what we have acoustically in terms of the amount of fish that came up in the net versus the amount of fish that was in the water, we get that information as we are catching, which is why we can talk to full pilchards, how big that is. The issue for us was not catching that amount, was not catching pilchards because

the amount of fish that was in the water was incredible at the time. What would cause a massive increase in pilchards? It could be environmental conditions. It could be just a recruitment event. So they've just had a massive event where they come together and spawn and just biologically produce a lot of fish. So there are a number of species, southern blue whiting is one, for example, that they come together once every sort of 6 to 10 years but then they have massive recruitment.

Just one more, this is really important. It's just would you then be wanting a temporary increase- Yes... or a permanent increase? If that's- And we'd just recognize the position you're at at the moment and say that that cannot stay for another year because in those events for something like pilchards, there's no guarantee that biomass would be there the following year. Mm-hmm. Because they do move a lot. Suz.

As you no doubt are aware, we've had a huge amount of interest and feedback from recreational fishers. And including from iwi interests and you guys have sort of got a foot in both camps. Yes. So they're concerned about we're exporting all our fish and we're not leaving enough here for people here and they're also concerned about the public scrutiny and the minister being able to increase the catch and I just wondered what your feedback is on that with as sort of having a foot in both camps. How do you feel?

I'm happy- Supportive of the bill. Well, I'm happy to give you my viewpoint. It is important to recognize sealord only fishes in the deep water, so we don't actually cross over into recreational areas-... because we're outside of the 26 mile and 12 mile line. Nice. But in saying that, obviously I'm part of the industry. I've been in it for 15 years. So look, I think the interest of everyone is to have bountiful fisheries.

And one of the things that you'll hear from inshore fishers and from rock lobster is the need to be able to make fine scale adjustments within the different QMAs because at the moment it's a very blunt tool of just reduce the TAC and a lot of times- Mm... you'll find people fighting to keep the TAC up because it's not an issue for the stock in totality.

Mm. So by having the ability to recognize all those fine scales, you can make targeted decisions and recognize the issues for recreational fishers as well, which in some sub areas actually it's difficult to catch fish. And therefore rather than drop a whole TAC, you say, "Well, we make some adjustments around that." I think that's an incredibly positive change coming out of the bill.

Thank you. Quick, Suz. Okay, very quickly, I note that you did support the 20 working day timeframe for the review. So other industry bodies haven't supported that, and essentially the concern was that it might incentivize legal action because there wasn't enough time to do anything else. How- I think that's a perspective that some have and look, for us, we believe 20 days is enough. If we want to take action, we believe we can analyze the data and do the stuff to be able to take action within the 20 days.

Look, it's not a big deal for us. We're not that concerned if it was a longer period of time, but we just analyze it from our own perspective. We believe if we were required to ask for a judicial review, we can do it within the 20 days. Okay. Thank you so much. Steve. Yeah, thank you. The sealord settlements are a vital part of the treaty settlements.

Yeah. Not contesting that. I guess there's a problem with public perception of the general honesty of the fishery- Mm... of the fishing industry broadly, of which you're a huge part. Yeah. One of the obvious examples is in the deep water, years of denying that you guys even touched the bottom until it was proved independently-... that you were trawling up massive chunks of deep water coral.

Do you therefore recognize the need for there to be a social license for you to operate, that there has to be greater scrutiny on what's actually happening? Because the fact is the commercial fishers have not been honest with the public about the impact they've had ecologically. Well, I think there's two things. I think one, the fishing industry is dramatically different to what it was 20 years ago and even as much as 10 years ago.

Particularly in the deep water because there's been a huge amount of consolidation and a lot of the operators that were around the times that you refer, they no longer operate. I think the addition of having observers onto most of the deep water boats so that we have a significant observer coverage, I think that should give the public real belief that now there's a lot of transparency.

The technology changes that have occurred in the last 5, really 5 to 8 years have also greatly increased the transparency. And look, Sealord's on record saying we support cameras-Obviously we've said, look, we don't want to pay for observers and cameras because essentially it would be unaffordable for us almost to go fishing.

But I don't have any issue with camera footage on boats, and for the regulator to be able to see that footage. Very good. So- Oh. Just quickly, you know of the call for an end to bottom trawling. Yeah, I do. What would be the practical implementation of that in the context of your- It'll close Sealord, essentially, yeah.

So 76- And how would that be managed in terms of your treaty rights? No, I think-... I think it would be in court. That's bottom trawling. Steve, it's not in the bill. It's not in the bill. I know, but we've got Sealord- Mac. Yeah. But we're talking about the bill. Mac. Yeah, hi team, hi Doug. Good to see you, mate. You got it, Mac. Yeah, killed it. Just interested in, you mentioned scientific lag.

I'm cognizant of that, and it's really hard to ameliorate the effects of that, given that, as you said, it could be a couple of years in fact. I mean, we understand that deepwater fishery is under a huge pressure, but I'm actually cognizant of the inshore commercial sector and how they deal with multiple species, which is often quite different from the bluewater stuff.

Mm-hmm. How do you think scientific lag affects them? And moreover, from non-regulatory tools that are implemented, do you think from a voluntary point of view, people are actually doing what they say they're doing? Well, I think the inshore is a much better example of them using non-regulatory tools more often.

I think the deepwater has come to it in a much later timeframe because they had science and they had the ability to move TACs more frequently than what you've seen in the inshore. So I think inshore actually have been ahead of the game for a long period of time of saying that the fishers are seeing an issue, and then making a change irregardless of what the TAC is and what it says. So I think actually they've been a very good example.

And you can find it in multiple fisheries in the inshore. Deepwater's now using a lot more of those things. So you're finding shelving, you're finding voluntary changes, notwithstanding the science. So hoki has the most money spent on it, and yet we took voluntary measures to drop TACs, shelve fish, and close areas.

Notwithstanding, we've got a lot of science. Look, thanks very much, Doug. We've come to time. I think the committee's going to disperse. Thank you. But I really appreciate you taking the time to come in and see us and the submission that you've put in. So thanks very much. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks, Doug. Thank you. So where are you going to be?
